title: Fragment. Written Shortly After The Marriage Of Miss Chaworth. [1]
content:
First published in Moore's 'Letters and Journals of Lord Byron', 1830, i. 56
1.
Hills of Annesley, Bleak and Barren,
Where my thoughtless Childhood stray'd,
How the northern Tempests, warring,
Howl above thy tufted Shade!
2.
Now no more, the Hours beguiling,
Former favourite Haunts I see;
Now no more my Mary smiling,
Makes ye seem a Heaven to Me.
meaning:
This evocative piece by George Gordon Byron, titled "Fragment. Written Shortly After The Marriage Of Miss Chaworth. [1]", represents a masterful exploration of classical-poetry. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
